steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student wants to compare Fodor’s hypothesis with Carruthers’s. The key difference in the notes: Fodor says low-level systems are modular but high-level ones are not, while Carruthers’s MMH extends modularity to all cognitive systems. A good comparison must name this scope difference accurately.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "Following Fodor's 1983 hypothesis, Carruthers proposed that modularity of mind includes all cognitive systems.": This says Carruthers followed Fodor without contrasting them — it reads as agreement, not a comparison of their differences. ✗
  • Choice B — "In 2003, Carruthers proposed the massive modularity hypothesis, disagreeing with Fodor's earlier hypothesis that the mind is composed of innate neural structures.": Both theorists agree that the mind contains innate neural structures. Their disagreement is about scope, not about innate structures. ✗
  • Choice C — "In considering some but not all cognitive systems modular, Fodorian modularity is not as expansive in its definition of modularity as MMH is.": This directly names the scope difference: Fodor covers only some systems, Carruthers’s MMH covers all — making MMH more expansive. That is an accurate comparison drawn from the notes. ✓
  • Choice D — "The hypotheses of Fodor and Carruthers differ in whether they consider low-level cognitive systems, such as perception and language, modular.": Both hypotheses agree that low-level systems are modular. The disagreement is about high-level systems. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
